title: Hire Two Engineers Now or Preserve Runway
question: Should we hire two additional engineers this quarter despite reducing runway?
context: The product roadmap has revenue-critical enterprise features, but cash discipline matters because the next fundraise timing is uncertain.

decision_frame.desired_outcome: Increase roadmap velocity while preserving enough runway to keep fundraising optionality.
constraints: Projected runway cannot fall below the internal minimum.
Second hire must be tied to revenue evidence.
non_goals: Do not solve every roadmap problem with headcount.
Do not hire below the seniority bar just to move faster.

recommendation.summary: Approve one senior engineering hire tied to enterprise revenue milestones and defer the second hire until pipeline conversion improves.

financial_hypothesis: One senior hire accelerates enterprise roadmap enough to improve conversion, while two hires create too much runway risk before revenue proof.
model_driver: Enterprise ARR conversion rate and monthly burn increase.
base_case: One hire increases burn moderately and helps ship two enterprise blockers within one quarter.
upside_case: Faster roadmap completion improves enterprise conversion and offsets the hire through incremental ARR.
downside_case: Enterprise conversion does not improve, burn rises, and fundraising pressure increases.
sensitivity_checks: Runway if revenue is delayed by three months.
Runway if hiring cost is 20% above plan.
ARR needed to offset the hire within two quarters.
financial_guardrails: Do not let projected runway fall below the internal minimum.
Tie second hire to enterprise conversion evidence.
Pause hiring if sales cycle length increases materially.

hypotheses.statement: The first senior hire has a high probability of accelerating revenue-critical roadmap work.
hypotheses.why_it_matters: If the hire unlocks enterprise deals, the burn increase is justified.
evidence: Enterprise prospects repeatedly request the same missing workflow features.
Current engineering team is capacity constrained.
assumptions: A strong senior hire can onboard quickly.
The requested features are truly blockers, not nice-to-have items.
counterarguments: Hiring may not translate into near-term shipping speed.
Sales may overstate feature importance when deals are uncertain.
disconfirming_signals: Prospects do not commit after feature delivery.

hypotheses.statement: Hiring two engineers before stronger revenue proof creates unnecessary runway risk.
hypotheses.why_it_matters: The company should preserve strategic flexibility until conversion improves.
evidence: Second hire has weaker direct linkage to immediate enterprise blockers.
assumptions: The team can sequence roadmap work with one hire.
Waiting one quarter will not cause the best candidates to disappear entirely.
counterarguments: Under-hiring could delay product velocity and cost revenue.
Two hires may reduce key-person risk in engineering.
disconfirming_signals: Roadmap slippage continues after one hire.
Enterprise prospects churn from the pipeline due to delivery delays.

options.name: Hire two engineers now
options.expected_value: High upside if roadmap velocity is the bottleneck.
options.cost: Two full compensation packages plus onboarding load.

options.name: Hire one engineer now
options.description: Add targeted capacity while preserving more runway.
options.expected_value: Balanced tradeoff between speed and cash control.
options.cost: One compensation package and onboarding load.

options.name: Defer all hiring
options.description: Preserve runway and rely on current team.
options.expected_value: Lowest cash risk but highest roadmap risk.
options.downside: Enterprise delivery may remain too slow.
options.cost: Opportunity cost from delayed product work.

evidence.claim: Enterprise blockers are concentrated in a small set of product gaps.
evidence.source: Sales notes and product discovery calls.
evidence.notes: Needs tagging by deal stage and ACV.

evidence.claim: Current engineering capacity is the roadmap bottleneck.
evidence.source: Sprint planning and roadmap slippage review.
evidence.notes: Should separate scope churn from true capacity shortage.

evidence.claim: Two hires reduce runway before revenue proof is strong enough.
evidence.notes: Model should be refreshed before offer approval.

assumption_register.assumption: Enterprise feature gaps are genuine blockers to conversion.
assumption_register.test: Map each blocker to named deals, expected ACV, and written customer confirmation where possible.

assumption_register.assumption: One senior hire can onboard fast enough to affect this quarter's roadmap.
assumption_register.test: Define first 30-day milestone and compare against current team delivery bottlenecks.

assumption_register.assumption: Fundraising optionality is more valuable than maximum near-term velocity.
assumption_register.test: Stress-test runway under delayed revenue and delayed fundraising scenarios.

risks.trigger: Feature delivery does not create signed commitments or late-stage conversion.
risks.mitigation: Approve one hire and tie second hire to conversion evidence.

risks.trigger: Milestone slippage continues after onboarding or blockers were misdiagnosed.
risks.mitigation: Define exact features and delivery milestones before hiring.

risks.trigger: Candidate pipeline pressure causes lowering the seniority bar.
risks.mitigation: Do not compromise seniority bar to fill quickly.

decision_criteria.name: Runway protection
decision_criteria.description: Runway remains above internal minimum after the hire.

decision_criteria.name: Revenue linkage
decision_criteria.description: Hire maps to enterprise features with measurable revenue linkage.

decision_criteria.name: Execution speed
decision_criteria.description: Decision improves roadmap velocity within the planning horizon.

option_scores.rationale: Two hires create the largest runway reduction before revenue proof.
Some revenue linkage exists, but the second hire has weaker direct mapping.
Fastest capacity increase if onboarding works.
One hire preserves more runway and keeps the second hire conditional.
The hire can be scoped to named enterprise blockers.
Improves velocity, though less than hiring two people at once.
Does not address revenue-linked roadmap blockers.
Likely leaves current capacity bottleneck unchanged.

what_would_change_my_mind: Updated model shows one hire still pushes runway below the internal minimum.
Enterprise customers commit in writing that delivery unlocks contracts.
Current team can ship blockers without new headcount.
open_questions: Which exact feature milestones must the hire own?
What ARR is realistically unlocked by these roadmap items?
What is the minimum runway threshold?
next_actions: Refresh runway model with one-hire and two-hire scenarios.
Map enterprise blockers to specific deals and ACV.

expected_signals: The hired engineer owns and ships the enterprise blocker milestones.
Pipeline conversion or written commitment quality improves after delivery.
failure_signals: Runway falls below threshold after updated model.
Feature delivery does not change pipeline behavior.
Second-hire pressure returns without conversion evidence.
review_questions: Did the hire accelerate the roadmap?
